full_response:
Let  

• S be the inertial “lab’’ frame in which the hub is originally spinning with constant angular speed ω.  
• S′ be the instantaneous rest–frame of a small element of the platform that is being built.  
• R be the (proper and lab-frame) length of every radial rod (“spoke’’).

1.  Length of the spokes  
   Each spoke is always directed radially.  
   The velocity of any element of a spoke is tangential, i.e. perpendicular to the spoke itself, so there is no Lorentz contraction along the radial direction.  
   Therefore the physical radius of the wheel is the same in every frame and stays equal to R both while the wheel is spinning and after it is stopped.  

2.  Proper length of the circumference while spinning  
   Consider an infinitesimal arc element dℓ lying along the rim at radius R.  
   In the lab frame S this element moves with tangential speed  

         v = ω R .  

   Because the motion is parallel to the element itself, its length in S is Lorentz–contracted relative to its proper length dℓ₀ measured in its own rest–frame S′:

         dℓ = dℓ₀ / γ ,         γ ≡ 1 / √(1 – v²/c²).

   The ordinary Euclidean geometry of the lab frame gives for the entire rim

         ∮ dℓ = 2π R .

   Hence the sum of the proper (rest-frame) lengths of all the arc elements is

         C_built = ∮ dℓ₀ = γ ∮ dℓ = γ (2π R).         (1)

   That is, the builders, who are co-moving with the rim, must put down a total proper length γ 2πR of platform planks in order to go completely around the circle.

3.  Situation after the wheel is braked to rest  
   After the structure is brought to a full stop every part of it is at rest in the lab frame.  
   • The radius is still R (spokes were never contracted).  
   • The geometrical circumference that now has to be covered is the ordinary Euclidean value

         C_needed = 2π R .                                               (2)

   The platform that was built, however, possesses the larger proper length (1):

         C_built = γ 2π R  (γ > 1) .

   In other words, the ring of material is too long by

         ΔC = C_built − C_needed = (γ − 1) 2π R .                       (3)

4.  What must happen  

   The excess length ΔC cannot simply disappear.  When the wheel is stopped one of two things must occur:

   • If the platform is weak in compression it will wrinkle / corrugate / buckle until the extra length is accommodated.  
   • If it is held rigidly to the spokes or is very stiff it will be placed under large compressive stress; if the stress exceeds the material strength the platform or the attachments will crack or tear.

   In any case the structure cannot remain perfectly flat and smoothly attached to the rim without mechanical failure.

\boxed{\text{When the spinning wheel is stopped the platform is longer than the required circumference by a factor }\gamma>1;\ \text{it will therefore have to buckle, wrinkle, or tear because it no longer fits around radius }R.}

Confidence: 83%
